{{ill|lt=Gōsha in modern ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=gōsha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} |創建 = Multiple theories |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = Lunar September 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Ibaraki#Japan|label=Komo Shrine|lat_deg=35|lat_min=52|lat_sec=15.5|lon_deg=140|lon_min=10|lon_sec=53.97|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=top|width=200|caption=}} {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-width=200|type=point}} }} '''Komo Shrine''' (こうもうじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Tatsugi, {{ill|lt=Tone|Q1203324|WD=Q1203324|1=Tone, Ibaraki}}, {{ill|lt=Kitasōma District|Q1076978|WD=Q1076978|1=Kitasōma District, Ibaraki}}, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|WD=Q83273|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha in Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former rank under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|lt=Gōsha in modern ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=gōsha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. === Overview === It stands in southern Ibaraki, on one part of the {{ill|lt=Inashiki Plateau|Q102282520|WD=Q102282520|1=Hitachi Plateau}}. It is slightly south between Tone town center and {{ill|lt=Ryūgasaki|Q844138|WD=Q844138|1=Ryūgasaki, Ibaraki}} city center. The {{ill|lt=Shin-Tone River|Q11501429|WD=Q11501429|1=Shin-Tone River}} flows south of the shrine grounds. It consists of two shrine buildings, Kado-no-miya and Oku-no-miya. The current Kado-no-miya honden was rebuilt in 1698 ({{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001|1=Genroku}} 11, {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}), and the Oku-no-miya honden in 1703 (Genroku 16). The Kado-no-miya honden is a Tone Town designated cultural property{{cite web |url=https://www.town.tone.ibaraki.jp/kanko-bunka/rekisibunka/shiseki-bunkazai/page000161.html|title=Tone Town official site: Komo Shrine Kado-no-miya (left) / Oku-no-miya (right) |accessdate=2025-11-08}}. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Ōkami|1=Mizuhanome}} * {{ill|lt=Haniyasu|Q9285919|WD=Q9285919|lt=Haniyamahime-no-Ōkami|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime}} * {{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|lt=Kagutsuchi-no-Ōkami|1=Kagu-tsuchi}} * {{ill|lt=Kanayamahiko|Q4387856|WD=Q4387856|lt=Kanayamahiko-no-Ōkami|1=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime}} * {{ill|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Ōkami|1=Ukanomitama}} * {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane-kō|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} * {{ill|lt=Hachiman|Q261637|WD=Q261637|lt=Honda-wake-no-Ōkami|1=Hachiman}} The main deity is Mizuhanome-no-Ōkami, a water goddess governing water as the source of life. Many other deities, including Haniyamahime-no-Ōkami, are also {{ill|lt=merged|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}} in the paired hall. === History === According to shrine tradition, it began in 288 BCE ({{ill|lt=Emperor Kōrei|Q313041|WD=Q313041|1=Emperor Kōrei}} 3) with enshrinement of Mizuhanome-no-Ōkami at the present Kado-no-miya site. Later, in 698 CE ({{ill|lt=Emperor Monmu|Q357246|WD=Q357246|1=Emperor Monmu}} 2), Haniyamahime-no-Ōkami was enshrined in the paired hall and the buildings were restored{{cite web |url=http://www.koumoujinja.jp/what_koumou.html|title=Komo Shrine official site|accessdate=2025-11-08}}. In the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}}, it was listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} as “Komo Shrine, one seat, Sōma District, {{ill|lt=Shimōsa Province|Q1053695|WD=Q1053695|1=Shimōsa Province}},” and was ranked a shikinaisha. From the {{ill|lt=medieval period|Q12554|WD=Q12554|1=Middle Ages}} onward it was also called “Bumma Daimyōjin,” gathering faith as a local water deity. Since the {{ill|lt=early modern period|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718|1=Early modern period}}, it has inherited worship for rain-prayer, rain-stopping, and abundant harvest as the “oldest water-deity shrine in {{ill|lt=Kantō|Q11655841|WD=Q11655841|1=Kantō}},” and remains revered as a local guardian deity. {{-}} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Mitsuchino Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Mitsuchino Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Shimōsa Province]] [[Category:茨城県の神社]] [[Category:利根町の歴史]] [[Category:利根町の建築物]]

[[ファイル:Komo Shrine Main ToneTown.jpg|280px]]
Kado-no-miya (top) and Oku-no-miya (bottom) |所在地 = Kado-no-miya: {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|WD=Q83273|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kitasōma District|Q1076978|WD=Q1076978|1=Kitasōma District, Ibaraki}}{{ill|lt=Tone|Q1203324|WD=Q1203324|1=Tone, Ibaraki}} Tatsugi 2184
Oku-no-miya: 882 Tatsugi, Tone, Kitasōma District, Ibaraki |位置 = Kado-no-miya:
{{Coord|35|52|12|N|140|10|27.28|E|region:JP-08_scale:5000|display=inline,title|name=Kado-no-miya}}
Oku-no-miya:
{{Coord|35|52|15.5|N|140|10|53.97|E|region:JP-08_scale:50000|display=inline|name=Oku-no-miya}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Ōkami|1=Mizuhanome}}
{{ill|lt=Haniyasu|Q9285919|WD=Q9285919|lt=Haniyamahime-no-Ōkami|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime}}
{{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|lt=Kagutsuchi-no-Ōkami|1=Kagu-tsuchi}}
{{ill|lt=Kanayamahiko|Q4387856|WD=Q4387856|lt=Kanayamahiko-no-Ōkami|1=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime}}
{{ill|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Ōkami|1=Ukanomitama}}
{{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}
{{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane-kō|1=Sugawara no Michizane}}
{{ill|lt=Hachiman|Q261637|WD=Q261637|lt=Honda-wake-no-Ōkami|1=Hachiman}} |神体 = |社格 = {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha in Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}
{{ill|lt=Gōsha in modern ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=gōsha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} |創建 = Multiple theories |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = Lunar September 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Ibaraki#Japan|label=Komo Shrine|lat_deg=35|lat_min=52|lat_sec=15.5|lon_deg=140|lon_min=10|lon_sec=53.97|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=top|width=200|caption=}} {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-width=200|type=point}} }} '''Komo Shrine''' (こうもうじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Tatsugi, {{ill|lt=Tone|Q1203324|WD=Q1203324|1=Tone, Ibaraki}}, {{ill|lt=Kitasōma District|Q1076978|WD=Q1076978|1=Kitasōma District, Ibaraki}}, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|WD=Q83273|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha in Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former rank under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|lt=Gōsha in modern ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=gōsha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. === Overview === It stands in southern Ibaraki, on one part of the {{ill|lt=Inashiki Plateau|Q102282520|WD=Q102282520|1=Hitachi Plateau}}. It is slightly south between Tone town center and {{ill|lt=Ryūgasaki|Q844138|WD=Q844138|1=Ryūgasaki, Ibaraki}} city center. The {{ill|lt=Shin-Tone River|Q11501429|WD=Q11501429|1=Shin-Tone River}} flows south of the shrine grounds. It consists of two shrine buildings, Kado-no-miya and Oku-no-miya. The current Kado-no-miya honden was rebuilt in 1698 ({{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001|1=Genroku}} 11, {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}), and the Oku-no-miya honden in 1703 (Genroku 16). The Kado-no-miya honden is a Tone Town designated cultural property{{cite web |url=https://www.town.tone.ibaraki.jp/kanko-bunka/rekisibunka/shiseki-bunkazai/page000161.html|title=Tone Town official site: Komo Shrine Kado-no-miya (left) / Oku-no-miya (right) |accessdate=2025-11-08}}. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Ōkami|1=Mizuhanome}} * {{ill|lt=Haniyasu|Q9285919|WD=Q9285919|lt=Haniyamahime-no-Ōkami|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime}} * {{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|lt=Kagutsuchi-no-Ōkami|1=Kagu-tsuchi}} * {{ill|lt=Kanayamahiko|Q4387856|WD=Q4387856|lt=Kanayamahiko-no-Ōkami|1=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime}} * {{ill|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Ōkami|1=Ukanomitama}} * {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane-kō|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} * {{ill|lt=Hachiman|Q261637|WD=Q261637|lt=Honda-wake-no-Ōkami|1=Hachiman}} The main deity is Mizuhanome-no-Ōkami, a water goddess governing water as the source of life. Many other deities, including Haniyamahime-no-Ōkami, are also {{ill|lt=merged|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}} in the paired hall. === History === According to shrine tradition, it began in 288 BCE ({{ill|lt=Emperor Kōrei|Q313041|WD=Q313041|1=Emperor Kōrei}} 3) with enshrinement of Mizuhanome-no-Ōkami at the present Kado-no-miya site. Later, in 698 CE ({{ill|lt=Emperor Monmu|Q357246|WD=Q357246|1=Emperor Monmu}} 2), Haniyamahime-no-Ōkami was enshrined in the paired hall and the buildings were restored{{cite web |url=http://www.koumoujinja.jp/what_koumou.html|title=Komo Shrine official site|accessdate=2025-11-08}}. In the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}}, it was listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} as “Komo Shrine, one seat, Sōma District, {{ill|lt=Shimōsa Province|Q1053695|WD=Q1053695|1=Shimōsa Province}},” and was ranked a shikinaisha. From the {{ill|lt=medieval period|Q12554|WD=Q12554|1=Middle Ages}} onward it was also called “Bumma Daimyōjin,” gathering faith as a local water deity. Since the {{ill|lt=early modern period|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718|1=Early modern period}}, it has inherited worship for rain-prayer, rain-stopping, and abundant harvest as the “oldest water-deity shrine in {{ill|lt=Kantō|Q11655841|WD=Q11655841|1=Kantō}},” and remains revered as a local guardian deity. {{-}} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Mitsuchino Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Mitsuchino Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Shimōsa Province]] [[Category:茨城県の神社]] [[Category:利根町の歴史]] [[Category:利根町の建築物]]

It is a {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha in Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former rank under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|lt=Gōsha in modern ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=gōsha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. === Overview === It stands in southern Ibaraki, on one part of the {{ill|lt=Inashiki Plateau|Q102282520|WD=Q102282520|1=Hitachi Plateau}}. It is slightly south between Tone town center and {{ill|lt=Ryūgasaki|Q844138|WD=Q844138|1=Ryūgasaki, Ibaraki}} city center. The {{ill|lt=Shin-Tone River|Q11501429|WD=Q11501429|1=Shin-Tone River}} flows south of the shrine grounds. It consists of two shrine buildings, Kado-no-miya and Oku-no-miya. The current Kado-no-miya honden was rebuilt in 1698 ({{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001|1=Genroku}} 11, {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}), and the Oku-no-miya honden in 1703 (Genroku 16). The Kado-no-miya honden is a Tone Town designated cultural property{{cite web |url=https://www.town.tone.ibaraki.jp/kanko-bunka/rekisibunka/shiseki-bunkazai/page000161.html|title=Tone Town official site: Komo Shrine Kado-no-miya (left) / Oku-no-miya (right) |accessdate=2025-11-08}}. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Ōkami|1=Mizuhanome}} * {{ill|lt=Haniyasu|Q9285919|WD=Q9285919|lt=Haniyamahime-no-Ōkami|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime}} * {{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|lt=Kagutsuchi-no-Ōkami|1=Kagu-tsuchi}} * {{ill|lt=Kanayamahiko|Q4387856|WD=Q4387856|lt=Kanayamahiko-no-Ōkami|1=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime}} * {{ill|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Ōkami|1=Ukanomitama}} * {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane-kō|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} * {{ill|lt=Hachiman|Q261637|WD=Q261637|lt=Honda-wake-no-Ōkami|1=Hachiman}} The main deity is Mizuhanome-no-Ōkami, a water goddess governing water as the source of life. Many other deities, including Haniyamahime-no-Ōkami, are also {{ill|lt=merged|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}} in the paired hall. === History === According to shrine tradition, it began in 288 BCE ({{ill|lt=Emperor Kōrei|Q313041|WD=Q313041|1=Emperor Kōrei}} 3) with enshrinement of Mizuhanome-no-Ōkami at the present Kado-no-miya site. Later, in 698 CE ({{ill|lt=Emperor Monmu|Q357246|WD=Q357246|1=Emperor Monmu}} 2), Haniyamahime-no-Ōkami was enshrined in the paired hall and the buildings were restored{{cite web |url=http://www.koumoujinja.jp/what_koumou.html|title=Komo Shrine official site|accessdate=2025-11-08}}. In the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}}, it was listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} as “Komo Shrine, one seat, Sōma District, {{ill|lt=Shimōsa Province|Q1053695|WD=Q1053695|1=Shimōsa Province}},” and was ranked a shikinaisha. From the {{ill|lt=medieval period|Q12554|WD=Q12554|1=Middle Ages}} onward it was also called “Bumma Daimyōjin,” gathering faith as a local water deity. Since the {{ill|lt=early modern period|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718|1=Early modern period}}, it has inherited worship for rain-prayer, rain-stopping, and abundant harvest as the “oldest water-deity shrine in {{ill|lt=Kantō|Q11655841|WD=Q11655841|1=Kantō}},” and remains revered as a local guardian deity. {{-}} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Mitsuchino Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Mitsuchino Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Shimōsa Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:利根町の歴史]] [[Category:利根町の建築物]]

Kado-no-miya (top) and Oku-no-miya (bottom) |所在地 = Kado-no-miya: {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|WD=Q83273|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kitasōma District|Q1076978|WD=Q1076978|1=Kitasōma District, Ibaraki}}{{ill|lt=Tone|Q1203324|WD=Q1203324|1=Tone, Ibaraki}} Tatsugi 2184
Oku-no-miya: 882 Tatsugi, Tone, Kitasōma District, Ibaraki |位置 = Kado-no-miya:
{{Coord|35|52|12|N|140|10|27.28|E|region:JP-08_scale:5000|display=inline,title|name=Kado-no-miya}}
Oku-no-miya:
{{Coord|35|52|15.5|N|140|10|53.97|E|region:JP-08_scale:50000|display=inline|name=Oku-no-miya}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Ōkami|1=Mizuhanome}}
{{ill|lt=Haniyasu|Q9285919|WD=Q9285919|lt=Haniyamahime-no-Ōkami|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime}}
{{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|lt=Kagutsuchi-no-Ōkami|1=Kagu-tsuchi}}
{{ill|lt=Kanayamahiko|Q4387856|WD=Q4387856|lt=Kanayamahiko-no-Ōkami|1=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime}}
{{ill|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Ōkami|1=Ukanomitama}}
{{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}
{{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane-kō|1=Sugawara no Michizane}}
{{ill|lt=Hachiman|Q261637|WD=Q261637|lt=Honda-wake-no-Ōkami|1=Hachiman}} |神体 = |社格 = {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha in Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}
{{ill|lt=Gōsha in modern ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=gōsha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} |創建 = Multiple theories |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = Lunar September 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Ibaraki#Japan|label=Komo Shrine|lat_deg=35|lat_min=52|lat_sec=15.5|lon_deg=140|lon_min=10|lon_sec=53.97|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=top|width=200|caption=}} {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-width=200|type=point}} }} '''Komo Shrine''' (こうもうじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Tatsugi, {{ill|lt=Tone|Q1203324|WD=Q1203324|1=Tone, Ibaraki}}, {{ill|lt=Kitasōma District|Q1076978|WD=Q1076978|1=Kitasōma District, Ibaraki}}, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|WD=Q83273|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha in Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former rank under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|lt=Gōsha in modern ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=gōsha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. === Overview === It stands in southern Ibaraki, on one part of the {{ill|lt=Inashiki Plateau|Q102282520|WD=Q102282520|1=Hitachi Plateau}}. It is slightly south between Tone town center and {{ill|lt=Ryūgasaki|Q844138|WD=Q844138|1=Ryūgasaki, Ibaraki}} city center. The {{ill|lt=Shin-Tone River|Q11501429|WD=Q11501429|1=Shin-Tone River}} flows south of the shrine grounds. It consists of two shrine buildings, Kado-no-miya and Oku-no-miya. The current Kado-no-miya honden was rebuilt in 1698 ({{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001|1=Genroku}} 11, {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}), and the Oku-no-miya honden in 1703 (Genroku 16). The Kado-no-miya honden is a Tone Town designated cultural property{{cite web |url=https://www.town.tone.ibaraki.jp/kanko-bunka/rekisibunka/shiseki-bunkazai/page000161.html|title=Tone Town official site: Komo Shrine Kado-no-miya (left) / Oku-no-miya (right) |accessdate=2025-11-08}}. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Ōkami|1=Mizuhanome}} * {{ill|lt=Haniyasu|Q9285919|WD=Q9285919|lt=Haniyamahime-no-Ōkami|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime}} * {{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|lt=Kagutsuchi-no-Ōkami|1=Kagu-tsuchi}} * {{ill|lt=Kanayamahiko|Q4387856|WD=Q4387856|lt=Kanayamahiko-no-Ōkami|1=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime}} * {{ill|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Ōkami|1=Ukanomitama}} * {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane-kō|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} * {{ill|lt=Hachiman|Q261637|WD=Q261637|lt=Honda-wake-no-Ōkami|1=Hachiman}} The main deity is Mizuhanome-no-Ōkami, a water goddess governing water as the source of life. Many other deities, including Haniyamahime-no-Ōkami, are also {{ill|lt=merged|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}} in the paired hall. === History === According to shrine tradition, it began in 288 BCE ({{ill|lt=Emperor Kōrei|Q313041|WD=Q313041|1=Emperor Kōrei}} 3) with enshrinement of Mizuhanome-no-Ōkami at the present Kado-no-miya site. Later, in 698 CE ({{ill|lt=Emperor Monmu|Q357246|WD=Q357246|1=Emperor Monmu}} 2), Haniyamahime-no-Ōkami was enshrined in the paired hall and the buildings were restored{{cite web |url=http://www.koumoujinja.jp/what_koumou.html|title=Komo Shrine official site|accessdate=2025-11-08}}. In the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}}, it was listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} as “Komo Shrine, one seat, Sōma District, {{ill|lt=Shimōsa Province|Q1053695|WD=Q1053695|1=Shimōsa Province}},” and was ranked a shikinaisha. From the {{ill|lt=medieval period|Q12554|WD=Q12554|1=Middle Ages}} onward it was also called “Bumma Daimyōjin,” gathering faith as a local water deity. Since the {{ill|lt=early modern period|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718|1=Early modern period}}, it has inherited worship for rain-prayer, rain-stopping, and abundant harvest as the “oldest water-deity shrine in {{ill|lt=Kantō|Q11655841|WD=Q11655841|1=Kantō}},” and remains revered as a local guardian deity. {{-}} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Mitsuchino Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Mitsuchino Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Shimōsa Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:利根町の歴史]] [[Category:利根町の建築物]]

{{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}
{{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane-kō|1=Sugawara no Michizane}}
{{ill|lt=Hachiman|Q261637|WD=Q261637|lt=Honda-wake-no-Ōkami|1=Hachiman}} |神体 = |社格 = {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha in Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}
{{ill|lt=Gōsha in modern ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=gōsha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} |創建 = Multiple theories |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = Lunar September 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Ibaraki#Japan|label=Komo Shrine|lat_deg=35|lat_min=52|lat_sec=15.5|lon_deg=140|lon_min=10|lon_sec=53.97|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=top|width=200|caption=}} {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-width=200|type=point}} }} '''Komo Shrine''' (こうもうじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Tatsugi, {{ill|lt=Tone|Q1203324|WD=Q1203324|1=Tone, Ibaraki}}, {{ill|lt=Kitasōma District|Q1076978|WD=Q1076978|1=Kitasōma District, Ibaraki}}, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|WD=Q83273|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha in Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former rank under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|lt=Gōsha in modern ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=gōsha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. === Overview === It stands in southern Ibaraki, on one part of the {{ill|lt=Inashiki Plateau|Q102282520|WD=Q102282520|1=Hitachi Plateau}}. It is slightly south between Tone town center and {{ill|lt=Ryūgasaki|Q844138|WD=Q844138|1=Ryūgasaki, Ibaraki}} city center. The {{ill|lt=Shin-Tone River|Q11501429|WD=Q11501429|1=Shin-Tone River}} flows south of the shrine grounds. It consists of two shrine buildings, Kado-no-miya and Oku-no-miya. The current Kado-no-miya honden was rebuilt in 1698 ({{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001|1=Genroku}} 11, {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}), and the Oku-no-miya honden in 1703 (Genroku 16). The Kado-no-miya honden is a Tone Town designated cultural property{{cite web |url=https://www.town.tone.ibaraki.jp/kanko-bunka/rekisibunka/shiseki-bunkazai/page000161.html|title=Tone Town official site: Komo Shrine Kado-no-miya (left) / Oku-no-miya (right) |accessdate=2025-11-08}}. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Ōkami|1=Mizuhanome}} * {{ill|lt=Haniyasu|Q9285919|WD=Q9285919|lt=Haniyamahime-no-Ōkami|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime}} * {{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|lt=Kagutsuchi-no-Ōkami|1=Kagu-tsuchi}} * {{ill|lt=Kanayamahiko|Q4387856|WD=Q4387856|lt=Kanayamahiko-no-Ōkami|1=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime}} * {{ill|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Ōkami|1=Ukanomitama}} * {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane-kō|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} * {{ill|lt=Hachiman|Q261637|WD=Q261637|lt=Honda-wake-no-Ōkami|1=Hachiman}} The main deity is Mizuhanome-no-Ōkami, a water goddess governing water as the source of life. Many other deities, including Haniyamahime-no-Ōkami, are also {{ill|lt=merged|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}} in the paired hall. === History === According to shrine tradition, it began in 288 BCE ({{ill|lt=Emperor Kōrei|Q313041|WD=Q313041|1=Emperor Kōrei}} 3) with enshrinement of Mizuhanome-no-Ōkami at the present Kado-no-miya site. Later, in 698 CE ({{ill|lt=Emperor Monmu|Q357246|WD=Q357246|1=Emperor Monmu}} 2), Haniyamahime-no-Ōkami was enshrined in the paired hall and the buildings were restored{{cite web |url=http://www.koumoujinja.jp/what_koumou.html|title=Komo Shrine official site|accessdate=2025-11-08}}. In the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}}, it was listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} as “Komo Shrine, one seat, Sōma District, {{ill|lt=Shimōsa Province|Q1053695|WD=Q1053695|1=Shimōsa Province}},” and was ranked a shikinaisha. From the {{ill|lt=medieval period|Q12554|WD=Q12554|1=Middle Ages}} onward it was also called “Bumma Daimyōjin,” gathering faith as a local water deity. Since the {{ill|lt=early modern period|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718|1=Early modern period}}, it has inherited worship for rain-prayer, rain-stopping, and abundant harvest as the “oldest water-deity shrine in {{ill|lt=Kantō|Q11655841|WD=Q11655841|1=Kantō}},” and remains revered as a local guardian deity. {{-}} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Mitsuchino Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Mitsuchino Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Shimōsa Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:利根町の歴史]] [[Category:Buildings in Tone]]

It is a {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha in Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former rank under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|lt=Gōsha in modern ranking system|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=gōsha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. === Overview === It stands in southern Ibaraki, on one part of the {{ill|lt=Inashiki Plateau|Q102282520|WD=Q102282520|1=Hitachi Plateau}}. It is slightly south between Tone town center and {{ill|lt=Ryūgasaki|Q844138|WD=Q844138|1=Ryūgasaki, Ibaraki}} city center. The {{ill|lt=Shin-Tone River|Q11501429|WD=Q11501429|1=Shin-Tone River}} flows south of the shrine grounds. It consists of two shrine buildings, Kado-no-miya and Oku-no-miya. The current Kado-no-miya honden was rebuilt in 1698 ({{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001|1=Genroku}} 11, {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}), and the Oku-no-miya honden in 1703 (Genroku 16). The Kado-no-miya honden is a Tone Town designated cultural property{{cite web |url=https://www.town.tone.ibaraki.jp/kanko-bunka/rekisibunka/shiseki-bunkazai/page000161.html|title=Tone Town official site: Komo Shrine Kado-no-miya (left) / Oku-no-miya (right) |accessdate=2025-11-08}}. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Ōkami|1=Mizuhanome}} * {{ill|lt=Haniyasu|Q9285919|WD=Q9285919|lt=Haniyamahime-no-Ōkami|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime}} * {{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|WD=Q1640658|lt=Kagutsuchi-no-Ōkami|1=Kagu-tsuchi}} * {{ill|lt=Kanayamahiko|Q4387856|WD=Q4387856|lt=Kanayamahiko-no-Ōkami|1=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime}} * {{ill|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Ōkami|1=Ukanomitama}} * {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|WD=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|WD=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane-kō|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} * {{ill|lt=Hachiman|Q261637|WD=Q261637|lt=Honda-wake-no-Ōkami|1=Hachiman}} The main deity is Mizuhanome-no-Ōkami, a water goddess governing water as the source of life. Many other deities, including Haniyamahime-no-Ōkami, are also {{ill|lt=merged|Q11412524|WD=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}} in the paired hall. === History === According to shrine tradition, it began in 288 BCE ({{ill|lt=Emperor Kōrei|Q313041|WD=Q313041|1=Emperor Kōrei}} 3) with enshrinement of Mizuhanome-no-Ōkami at the present Kado-no-miya site. Later, in 698 CE ({{ill|lt=Emperor Monmu|Q357246|WD=Q357246|1=Emperor Monmu}} 2), Haniyamahime-no-Ōkami was enshrined in the paired hall and the buildings were restored{{cite web |url=http://www.koumoujinja.jp/what_koumou.html|title=Komo Shrine official site|accessdate=2025-11-08}}. In the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292|1=Heian period}}, it was listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} as “Komo Shrine, one seat, Sōma District, {{ill|lt=Shimōsa Province|Q1053695|WD=Q1053695|1=Shimōsa Province}},” and was ranked a shikinaisha. From the {{ill|lt=medieval period|Q12554|WD=Q12554|1=Middle Ages}} onward it was also called “Bumma Daimyōjin,” gathering faith as a local water deity. Since the {{ill|lt=early modern period|Q5308718|WD=Q5308718|1=Early modern period}}, it has inherited worship for rain-prayer, rain-stopping, and abundant harvest as the “oldest water-deity shrine in {{ill|lt=Kantō|Q11655841|WD=Q11655841|1=Kantō}},” and remains revered as a local guardian deity. {{-}} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Mitsuchino Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Mitsuchino Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Shimōsa Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Tone]] [[Category:Buildings in Tone]]

Kado-no-miya (top) and Oku-no-miya (bottom) |所在地 = Kado-no-miya: {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|qid=Q83273|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kitasōma District|Q1076978|qid=Q1076978|1=Kitasōma District, Ibaraki}}{{ill|lt=Tone|Q1203324|qid=Q1203324|1=Tone, Ibaraki}} Tatsugi 2184
Oku-no-miya: 882 Tatsugi, Tone, Kitasōma District, Ibaraki |位置 = Kado-no-miya:

Oku-no-miya:
{{Coord|35|52|15.5|N|140|10|53.97|E|region:JP-08_scale:50000|display=inline|name=Oku-no-miya}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|qid=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Ōkami|1=Mizuhanome}}
{{ill|lt=Haniyasu|Q9285919|qid=Q9285919|lt=Haniyamahime-no-Ōkami|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime}}
{{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|qid=Q1640658|lt=Kagutsuchi-no-Ōkami|1=Kagu-tsuchi}}
{{ill|lt=Kanayamahiko|Q4387856|qid=Q4387856|lt=Kanayamahiko-no-Ōkami|1=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime}}
{{ill|qid=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|qid=Q3080728|qid=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Ōkami|1=Ukanomitama}}
{{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|qid=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}
{{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|qid=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane-kō|1=Sugawara no Michizane}}
{{ill|lt=Hachiman|Q261637|qid=Q261637|lt=Honda-wake-no-Ōkami|1=Hachiman}} |神体 = |社格 = {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha in Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}
{{ill|lt=Gōsha in modern ranking system|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=gōsha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} |創建 = Multiple theories |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = Lunar September 15 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Ibaraki#Japan|label=Komo Shrine|lat_deg=35|lat_min=52|lat_sec=15.5|lon_deg=140|lon_min=10|lon_sec=53.97|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=top|width=200|caption=}} {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-width=200|type=point}} }} '''Komo Shrine''' (こうもうじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Tatsugi, {{ill|lt=Tone|Q1203324|qid=Q1203324|1=Tone, Ibaraki}}, {{ill|lt=Kitasōma District|Q1076978|qid=Q1076978|1=Kitasōma District, Ibaraki}}, {{ill|lt=Ibaraki Prefecture|Q83273|qid=Q83273|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha in Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former rank under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|qid=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|lt=Gōsha in modern ranking system|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=gōsha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. === Overview === It stands in southern Ibaraki, on one part of the {{ill|lt=Inashiki Plateau|Q102282520|qid=Q102282520|1=Hitachi Plateau}}. It is slightly south between Tone town center and {{ill|lt=Ryūgasaki|Q844138|qid=Q844138|1=Ryūgasaki, Ibaraki}} city center. The {{ill|lt=Shin-Tone River|Q11501429|qid=Q11501429|1=Shin-Tone River}} flows south of the shrine grounds. It consists of two shrine buildings, Kado-no-miya and Oku-no-miya. The current Kado-no-miya honden was rebuilt in 1698 ({{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|qid=Q1325001|1=Genroku}} 11, {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|qid=Q184963|1=Edo period}}), and the Oku-no-miya honden in 1703 (Genroku 16). The Kado-no-miya honden is a Tone Town designated cultural property{{cite web |url=https://www.town.tone.ibaraki.jp/kanko-bunka/rekisibunka/shiseki-bunkazai/page000161.html|title=Tone Town official site: Komo Shrine Kado-no-miya (left) / Oku-no-miya (right) |accessdate=2025-11-08}}. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|qid=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Ōkami|1=Mizuhanome}} * {{ill|lt=Haniyasu|Q9285919|qid=Q9285919|lt=Haniyamahime-no-Ōkami|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime}} * {{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|qid=Q1640658|lt=Kagutsuchi-no-Ōkami|1=Kagu-tsuchi}} * {{ill|lt=Kanayamahiko|Q4387856|qid=Q4387856|lt=Kanayamahiko-no-Ōkami|1=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime}} * {{ill|qid=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|qid=Q3080728|qid=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Ōkami|1=Ukanomitama}} * {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|qid=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|qid=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane-kō|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} * {{ill|lt=Hachiman|Q261637|qid=Q261637|lt=Honda-wake-no-Ōkami|1=Hachiman}} The main deity is Mizuhanome-no-Ōkami, a water goddess governing water as the source of life. Many other deities, including Haniyamahime-no-Ōkami, are also {{ill|lt=merged|Q11412524|qid=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}} in the paired hall. === History === According to shrine tradition, it began in 288 BCE ({{ill|lt=Emperor Kōrei|Q313041|qid=Q313041|1=Emperor Kōrei}} 3) with enshrinement of Mizuhanome-no-Ōkami at the present Kado-no-miya site. Later, in 698 CE ({{ill|lt=Emperor Monmu|Q357246|qid=Q357246|1=Emperor Monmu}} 2), Haniyamahime-no-Ōkami was enshrined in the paired hall and the buildings were restored{{cite web |url=http://www.koumoujinja.jp/what_koumou.html|title=Komo Shrine official site|accessdate=2025-11-08}}. In the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|qid=Q193292|1=Heian period}}, it was listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} as “Komo Shrine, one seat, Sōma District, {{ill|lt=Shimōsa Province|Q1053695|qid=Q1053695|1=Shimōsa Province}},” and was ranked a shikinaisha. From the {{ill|lt=medieval period|Q12554|qid=Q12554|1=Middle Ages}} onward it was also called “Bumma Daimyōjin,” gathering faith as a local water deity. Since the {{ill|lt=early modern period|Q5308718|qid=Q5308718|1=Early modern period}}, it has inherited worship for rain-prayer, rain-stopping, and abundant harvest as the “oldest water-deity shrine in {{ill|lt=Kantō|Q11655841|qid=Q11655841|1=Kantō}},” and remains revered as a local guardian deity. {{-}} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Mitsuchino Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Mitsuchino Shrine]][[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Shimōsa Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Tone]] [[Category:Buildings in Tone]]

It is a {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha in Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former rank under the {{ill|lt=Modern shrine ranking system|Q712534|qid=Q712534|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was {{ill|lt=Gōsha in modern ranking system|Q712534|qid=Q712534|lt=gōsha|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}. === Overview === It stands in southern Ibaraki, on one part of the {{ill|lt=Inashiki Plateau|Q102282520|qid=Q102282520|1=Hitachi Plateau}}. It is slightly south between Tone town center and {{ill|lt=Ryūgasaki|Q844138|qid=Q844138|1=Ryūgasaki, Ibaraki}} city center. The {{ill|lt=Shin-Tone River|Q11501429|qid=Q11501429|1=Shin-Tone River}} flows south of the shrine grounds. It consists of two shrine buildings, Kado-no-miya and Oku-no-miya. The current Kado-no-miya honden was rebuilt in 1698 ({{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|qid=Q1325001|1=Genroku}} 11, {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|qid=Q184963|1=Edo period}}), and the Oku-no-miya honden in 1703 (Genroku 16). The Kado-no-miya honden is a Tone Town designated cultural property{{cite web |url=https://www.town.tone.ibaraki.jp/kanko-bunka/rekisibunka/shiseki-bunkazai/page000161.html|title=Tone Town official site: Komo Shrine Kado-no-miya (left) / Oku-no-miya (right) |accessdate=2025-11-08}}. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|qid=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome-no-Ōkami|1=Mizuhanome}} * {{ill|lt=Haniyasu|Q9285919|qid=Q9285919|lt=Haniyamahime-no-Ōkami|1=Haniyasu-hiko and Haniyasu-hime}} * {{ill|lt=Kagutsuchi|Q1640658|qid=Q1640658|lt=Kagutsuchi-no-Ōkami|1=Kagu-tsuchi}} * {{ill|lt=Kanayamahiko|Q4387856|qid=Q4387856|lt=Kanayamahiko-no-Ōkami|1=Kanayama-hiko and Kanayama-hime}} * {{ill|qid=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|qid=Q3080728|qid=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama-no-Ōkami|1=Ukanomitama}} * {{ill|lt=Susanoo|Q272993|qid=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} * {{ill|lt=Sugawara no Michizane|Q382005|qid=Q382005|lt=Sugawara no Michizane-kō|1=Sugawara no Michizane}} * {{ill|lt=Hachiman|Q261637|qid=Q261637|lt=Honda-wake-no-Ōkami|1=Hachiman}} The main deity is Mizuhanome-no-Ōkami, a water goddess governing water as the source of life. Many other deities, including Haniyamahime-no-Ōkami, are also {{ill|lt=merged|Q11412524|qid=Q11412524|1=Co-Enshrinement}} in the paired hall. === History === According to shrine tradition, it began in 288 BCE ({{ill|lt=Emperor Kōrei|Q313041|qid=Q313041|1=Emperor Kōrei}} 3) with enshrinement of Mizuhanome-no-Ōkami at the present Kado-no-miya site. Later, in 698 CE ({{ill|lt=Emperor Monmu|Q357246|qid=Q357246|1=Emperor Monmu}} 2), Haniyamahime-no-Ōkami was enshrined in the paired hall and the buildings were restored{{cite web |url=http://www.koumoujinja.jp/what_koumou.html|title=Komo Shrine official site|accessdate=2025-11-08}}. In the {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|qid=Q193292|1=Heian period}}, it was listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}} as “Komo Shrine, one seat, Sōma District, {{ill|lt=Shimōsa Province|Q1053695|qid=Q1053695|1=Shimōsa Province}},” and was ranked a shikinaisha. From the {{ill|lt=medieval period|Q12554|qid=Q12554|1=Middle Ages}} onward it was also called “Bumma Daimyōjin,” gathering faith as a local water deity. Since the {{ill|lt=early modern period|Q5308718|qid=Q5308718|1=Early modern period}}, it has inherited worship for rain-prayer, rain-stopping, and abundant harvest as the “oldest water-deity shrine in {{ill|lt=Kantō|Q11655841|qid=Q11655841|1=Kantō}},” and remains revered as a local guardian deity. {{-}} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Mitsuchino Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Mitsuchino Shrine]][[Category:Disputed Shikinaisha or Shikigeisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Shimōsa Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ibaraki Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Tone]] [[Category:Buildings in Tone]]
